mnemonics: Member Operator Magic: Make My Operators Members (for modifying the object itself)
Left-Hand Loyalty: Member operators assume 'this' is the left operand.
Friend or Foe? For symmetric operators (like `2 + obj`), you'll need a friend/non-member function.
keyPoints: A member operator function is declared inside the class with the syntax: `ReturnType operator[Symbol](const ClassName& rhs);`
The left-hand operand (the one on the left side of the operator) is implicitly the current object (`*this`).
Member operator functions have direct access to the private and protected members of the class.
commonMistakes: Attempting to overload an operator where the first operand is a fundamental type (e.g., `int + MyClass`) as a member function, which is impossible.
Forgetting to make the operator function `const` when it should not modify the left-hand operand (e.g., in comparison operators like `==`).
Using the wrong return type, such as returning `void` from the assignment operator (`=`) instead of a reference to `*this` to allow chaining (`a = b = c`).
